fact: September 3 is exactly 246 days into the year, which means there are only 119 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and that's why you suddenly see them everywhere indoors!
The sun rises about 2 minutes later each day around September 3, so if you're trying to wake up early, blame the shrinking daylight!
September 3 falls smack in the middle of Virgo season, making it one of the most 'perfectionist' days of the year according to astrology fans.
Monarch butterflies are typically in full migration mode around this time in North America, with some flying over 2,000 miles - that's like flying from New York to Denver without stopping!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, September 3 is actually early spring, so flowers are just starting to bloom while the north gets ready for fall!
The autumn equinox is only about 19 days away from September 3, meaning day and night are getting closer to being exactly equal length.
Squirrels go absolutely nuts around this time of year in northern climates, burying up to 10,000 acorns each to prepare for winter - and they forget where half of them are!
September 3 marks the peak time when maple trees start pumping sugar down to their roots, which is what makes their leaves turn those amazing red and orange colors.
If you live in northern places like Canada or Alaska, you might start seeing the northern lights more clearly around this date as the nights get darker earlier.
Crickets typically chirp slower as temperatures drop in early September - you can actually estimate the temperature by counting their chirps and doing math!
September 3 is usually prime harvest time for apples in many northern regions, and a single apple tree can produce enough fruit to make 20 pies!
